verses.text: Joseph is brought down to Egypt. Potiphar, an Egyptian, captain of Pharaoh's guard, buys him from the Ishmaelites. The favored son is now an enslaved man. But the chapter is about to make a phrase repeat.

The LORD was with Joseph, and he was a prosperous man — in the house of his Egyptian master. His master saw that the LORD was with him and made everything he did prosper. The chapter starts and ends with the same line: the LORD was with him.

The LORD was with Joseph, and he was a prosperous man — in the house of his Egyptian master. His master saw that the LORD was with him and made everything he did prosper. The chapter starts and ends with the same line: the LORD was with him.

Joseph finds favor and is given charge over the whole household. The LORD blesses Potiphar's house for Joseph's sake. Potiphar leaves everything in Joseph's hand — he doesn't even know what he owns, except the bread he eats. Joseph is described as well-built and handsome. The detail will matter in a moment.

Joseph finds favor and is given charge over the whole household. The LORD blesses Potiphar's house for Joseph's sake. Potiphar leaves everything in Joseph's hand — he doesn't even know what he owns, except the bread he eats. Joseph is described as well-built and handsome. The detail will matter in a moment.

Joseph finds favor and is given charge over the whole household. The LORD blesses Potiphar's house for Joseph's sake. Potiphar leaves everything in Joseph's hand — he doesn't even know what he owns, except the bread he eats. Joseph is described as well-built and handsome. The detail will matter in a moment.

After all this, Potiphar's wife begins to look at Joseph. She speaks one short line: lie with me. The text doesn't soften her language. She is the master; he is the slave. The power flows the wrong direction.

Joseph refuses with two reasons. First, the trust: my master has kept nothing from me except you, because you are his wife. Second, the deeper one: how can I do this great wickedness and sin against God? He names the act as sin, not just betrayal.

Joseph refuses with two reasons. First, the trust: my master has kept nothing from me except you, because you are his wife. Second, the deeper one: how can I do this great wickedness and sin against God? He names the act as sin, not just betrayal.

She spoke to him day by day. He didn't hearken to her, to lie with her, or even to be with her. He doesn't only avoid the act — he avoids the proximity. Wisdom keeps its distance, not just its boundaries.

One day, none of the household men are in the house. She catches him by his garment: lie with me. He leaves his garment in her hand and runs. The price of holding his integrity is being half-dressed and accused. Sometimes obedience looks like flight.

One day, none of the household men are in the house. She catches him by his garment: lie with me. He leaves his garment in her hand and runs. The price of holding his integrity is being half-dressed and accused. Sometimes obedience looks like flight.

When she sees the garment in her hand, she calls the men of the house: look, my husband has brought in this Hebrew to mock us. He came in to lie with me. I cried out, and he fled and left his garment. She turns her assault into his crime by holding the evidence and changing the story.

When she sees the garment in her hand, she calls the men of the house: look, my husband has brought in this Hebrew to mock us. He came in to lie with me. I cried out, and he fled and left his garment. She turns her assault into his crime by holding the evidence and changing the story.

When she sees the garment in her hand, she calls the men of the house: look, my husband has brought in this Hebrew to mock us. He came in to lie with me. I cried out, and he fled and left his garment. She turns her assault into his crime by holding the evidence and changing the story.

She keeps the garment with her until Potiphar comes home, then tells him the same story. The Hebrew servant you brought us came in to mock me; when I cried out, he fled and left his garment. The trap closes.

She keeps the garment with her until Potiphar comes home, then tells him the same story. The Hebrew servant you brought us came in to mock me; when I cried out, he fled and left his garment. The trap closes.

She keeps the garment with her until Potiphar comes home, then tells him the same story. The Hebrew servant you brought us came in to mock me; when I cried out, he fled and left his garment. The trap closes.

Potiphar hears and his anger is kindled. He takes Joseph and puts him in prison — the place where the king's prisoners are held. Notice: he doesn't have Joseph killed, which an actual rape charge would have demanded. Some part of him may know.

Potiphar hears and his anger is kindled. He takes Joseph and puts him in prison — the place where the king's prisoners are held. Notice: he doesn't have Joseph killed, which an actual rape charge would have demanded. Some part of him may know.

But the LORD was with Joseph and showed him mercy. He gave him favor in the eyes of the prison keeper. The same phrase that began the chapter — the LORD was with him — finds Joseph again at the bottom.

The prison keeper puts everything under Joseph's hand, just like Potiphar did. Whatever Joseph does, the LORD makes it prosper. He is rising even while imprisoned. God's presence travels with him through every demotion.
